name: Eunsaem Cho
desc: Snowmelt-Groundwater Interactions for Improved Flood Prediction Using Land Surface Modeling and Data Assimilation
about: Dr. Eunsaem Cho is a hydrologist whose research advances water resources management, hydroclimate extreme prediction, and resilient infrastructure design in a changing climate. Currently, his work focuses on understanding the influence of groundwater on snowmelt-driven floods through retrospective simulations with the Noah-MP land surface model in NASA’s Land Information System and GRACE/GRACE-FO data assimilation. He is currently a Postdoctoral Associate at the Hydrological Sciences Laboratory, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, and at the Earth System Science Interdisciplinary Center, University of Maryland. Previously, he was a Postdoctoral Associate at Florida State University (2022–2024), focusing on compound flood risk assessment, and a Research Professor at Korea University (2020–2022), studying urban hydrology.

name: Harisankar Manoj
desc: Investigating land–climate interactions using remote sensing of snow and floods
about: Harisankar’s research interests include the climate implications associated with land surface processes, the use of remote sensing for snow estimation, and snowmelt-driven floods. He worked as a GIS analyst at Apple via Artech Information Systems LLC, where he improved the Apple Maps Product Automation through geospatial data analysis and quality control – quality assurance. Prior to his industrial experience, Harisankar completed his master's degree in geological sciences from the University of Texas at Austin, where he investigated whether climate alterations are more or less able to support the sustenance of Congo rainforests. He earned his integrated master’s degree in applied geology from Pondicherry University, India, where he studied the lithological control on weathering and gained experience in geological fieldwork, as well as geochemical analysis of soil, sediment, and rock specimens. Besides his career interests, Harisankar is an avid music listener; he enjoys playing the violin, photography, and exploring nature.

activities.desc: Attended and presented at *AMS 2026*, Houston, Texas, USA.

name: Kathleen Shank
desc: Integrating climate and hydrologic models to estimate future changes in PFAS contaminant behavior
about: Kathleen’s research focuses on the intersection of climate and hydrology as climate stressors influence water quality and contaminant behavior (i.e., PFAS). She hopes to tailor her research based on her previous experience in sea level rise, coastal forest ecology, and water quality. Kathleen is passionate about the environment and climate adaptation in communities with lower adaptive capacity to climate change. She hopes her research can help communities better prepare for future climate stressors and increase awareness of the public health impacts of climate change. Prior to graduate school, Kathleen worked at the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s Office of Research and Development, where she researched coastal wetland inundation with future sea level rise in the U.S. and its island territories, focusing on mangrove forests and saltwater marshes. Kathleen completed her undergraduate degree from Penn State University in Earth Science and Policy, concentrating her studies in climate change. When she is not deep in her research, Kathleen enjoys hiking, painting, and live music.

activities.desc: Attended and presented at *AMS 2026*, Houston, Texas, USA.

ConferencePapers.desc: *Shank, K. I.*, Cho, E., & Hwang, S. S. (2026). Identifying PFAS Hotspots Influenced by Hydrologic and Climatic Stressors Using Remote Sensing and Geospatial Analysis. Poster presented at the *AMS Annual Meeting 2026*, Houston, TX, USA.

name: Annelise Holguin
desc: Crop Yield Predictions using ML and remote sensing
about: Annelise’s research interests include remote sensing, hydrology, human-environmental impacts, and social justice. Currently her work in the Cho Lab utilizes remote sensing and machine learning to predict crop yield using a specialized database from the U.S. Department of Agriculture’s Beltsville Agricultural Research Center. She is currently working on her M.S. in Geography at Texas State University, where she also attended undergraduate studies in Geography Resource Use and Environmental Studies. When she’s not in the lab, she likes to represent the university on the swim club and in choir.

name: Farhaan Cooverji
desc: Regional Water Balance Analysis for Quantifying Snow Water Supply in Snow-Dominant Watersheds.
about: Farhaan’s research interests are centered around the cryosphere, integrating remote sensing, land surface modeling, and GIS to investigate snow and ice processes. His current work examines snow-induced runoff in the Smith River Watershed (Montana, U.S.) by combining land surface model outputs, ground-based observations, and water balance analyses. He graduated with a double major in Anthropology and GIS from Texas State University and has returned to pursue an M.S. in Geography. Beyond research, Farhaan serves as an assistant coach for the TXST Men’s Soccer Club and enjoys exploring media regarding archaeology and world history.

name: Jipeng Liu
desc: UAV remote sensing of snow and soil moisture detections.
about: Jipeng is a Master’s student in Civil Engineering at Texas State University. His research bridges hydrology, remote sensing, and UAV-based geospatial technologies, with a focus on improving snow depth and soil moisture measurements. His current projects include evaluating the accuracy of airborne LiDAR for snow depth estimation in boreal forests using NASA SnowEx 2023 data and developing UAV-mounted gamma-ray spectroscopy approaches for soil moisture monitoring. He also holds a Part 107 FAA Remote Pilot Certificate, which supports his expertise in UAV-based environmental observation. Beyond research, he serves as a Graduate Instructional Assistant for the Ingram School of Engineering, assisting in Fluid Mechanics courses. In addition to his academic pursuits, Jipeng is the President of the Chinese Students and Scholars Association at Texas State University, where he leads cultural, academic, and community-building initiatives. He earned his Bachelor’s degree in Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering from Shandong Agricultural University (2020–2024). He also enjoys teaching, collaborating with peers in the Journal Club, and engaging with innovative applications of UAV technology in water resources science.

name: Md S.M.I Momin
desc: Impact of Coastal Flooding on Transportation Systems and Social Equity

activities.label: Training

about: Momin is a water resources and transportation researcher whose work focuses on the impacts of coastal flooding on roadway networks and infrastructure resilience. He integrates hydrodynamic modeling, GIS, and traffic simulation to evaluate how extreme coastal flood events affect transportation systems. Momin earned his B.Sc. in Civil Engineering from the Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology (BUET), one of the premier engineering institutions in South Asia, and went on to serve in the Bangladesh Water Development Board, where he designed hydraulic infrastructures, managed river basin projects, and implemented flood control and monitoring initiatives. At Texas State University, his research includes developing flood hazard maps for Galveston Island, simulating the 1-in-100 AEP flood event and Hurricane Ike 2008, and identifying vulnerable roadway segments using macroscopic traffic simulation, while also applying advanced machine learning and deep learning techniques—including LSTM, GRU, CNN-LSTM, and Transformer models—for forecasting sea water levels and assessing flood risk. Beyond the U.S., Momin has investigated riverbank line shifting along the Meghna River in Bhola Island, Bangladesh, and presented research on structural performance evaluation at the International Conference on Disaster Risk Mitigation (BUET-JIDPUS). His academic excellence has been recognized with awards, including second place at the TXST STEM Conference 2025 and the Graduate Merit Fellowship at Texas State University. Through his work, Momin aims to advance climate-resilient transportation planning by providing evidence-based solutions that strengthen roadway durability and promote equity in flood-prone coastal regions.

name: Samar Ranjit
desc: Undergraduate researcher passionate about machine learning. Contributing to crop yield stability studies at the Cho Lab.
about: Samar Ranjit is an undergraduate student at Texas State University with a strong interest in data science, remote sensing, and the ways technology can be applied to environmental research. He is particularly drawn to how geospatial tools and advanced analytics can be used to study natural systems and address issues such as food security and climate resilience. At the Cho Lab, his work centers on analyzing crop yield dynamics, where he studies spatial patterns, detects yield breakpoints, and evaluates edge effects across agricultural fields to better understand yield stability over time. Beyond this research, Samar is actively developing his knowledge in artificial intelligence and machine learning, fields he sees as offering vast opportunities for solving complex, real-world problems. He approaches his academic and professional journey as one of continuous learning, always looking to build new skills and improve as a researcher. In the long term, his goal is to integrate computer science and Earth sciences, creating innovative tools and approaches that can advance sustainable agriculture and contribute to broader environmental solutions.

awards.desc: *President's List Honoree* (Fall 2024; Spring, Fall 2025, Texas State University)

name: Hrishant Sharma
desc: River Flow prediction using LSTM deep learning models , with a strong interest in data science.
about: Hrishant is an undergraduate majoring in Computer Science at Texas State University. His work focuses on machine-learning–based streamflow prediction across short- to sub-seasonal lead times. He builds end-to-end data workflows that combine Python data processing with remote-sensing features to produce reliable, reproducible pipelines for hydrology applications. On the modeling side, Hrishant evaluates LSTM, CNN-LSTM, and Transformer architectures, integrating hydro-meteorological inputs such as precipitation, temperature, snow metrics, and historical flow. He is also developing skills in cloud platforms and data-engineering practices to support scalable training and deployment. Passionate about data quality, he specializes in preprocessing, cleaning, and managing large datasets to ensure robust, interpretable results. Looking ahead, he aims to build cloud-ready data systems that translate research models into practical decision support for water-resources management. He is eager to keep learning and to deepen his data-engineering and research skill set.

activities.desc: Attended and presented at *AMS 2026*, Houston, Texas, USA.

awards.desc: *Undergraduate Research Fellowship* (2025, Texas State University)
